metadata.title: Global Oil Market Tracker — Iran-Israel Conflict
metadata.methodology: Oil prices reflect front-month futures contract settlements. Production figures use the most recent EIA and OPEC reporting. Consumer fuel prices are national weighted averages from AAA and EIA weekly surveys. All data sourced from official government and international agency publications.
metadata.disclaimer: Oil market data is sourced from the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), OPEC, and the International Energy Agency (IEA). Production figures and reserves data reflect the most recently published official statistics. Prices and consumer impact figures are based on published government data. Click source links to verify all figures independently.

Day 16 PM — Brent $105.66, WTI $101.32; oil tops $105 on Hormuz tensions
Day 17 — Brent $104.73, WTI $99.68; Dubai airport struck, Fujairah hit again, Fed meeting looms
Day 17 PM — Oil drops sharply: Brent -4.3%, WTI -6.1% after Bessent says US allowing Iranian tankers through Hormuz

Day 20 PM — Brent spikes $119 on Ras Laffan strike, settles $108.65; 7-nation Hormuz coalition; QatarEnergy 17% LNG offline

keyPlayers.warImpact: World's largest producer at record 13.5 Mbpd. SPR at 415 million barrels (58% capacity). Domestic production unaffected but refineries face higher input costs from Hormuz disruption. EIA forecasts production holding near record levels through 2026.

Key OPEC swing producer. Has 2.5 Mbpd spare capacity but Hormuz blockade prevents Gulf exports via traditional routes. Rerouting via Red Sea pipeline (East-West Pipeline, 5 Mbpd capacity) but Houthi threats limit throughput. Ras Tanura terminal inactive.

Production unaffected by Middle East conflict. Benefiting from higher prices despite Western sanctions. Expanding exports to China and India via ESPO pipeline and Kozmino port. Urals crude discount narrowing as global supply tightens.

Pre-conflict production was 3.26 Mbpd (crude). Under active military strikes since Feb 28. Oil infrastructure targeted in both June 2025 Twelve-Day War and current 2026 operations. Exports primarily to China (~80% of volume). Production declining under sustained air campaign. Kharg Island export terminal status uncertain.

Shut down 1.5 Mbpd of production due to Hormuz crisis. Southern Basra exports severely disrupted by Strait closure. Northern Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline (Turkey) operating at reduced capacity. Iranian-backed militia activity ongoing. Production was 4.01 Mbpd pre-crisis.

Fujairah port (outside Hormuz) partially operational but under Houthi missile threat. ADNOC Habshan-Fujairah pipeline (1.5 Mbpd capacity) is only viable export route. Jebel Ali port closed. Abu Dhabi production maintained but export bottlenecked.

Entirely dependent on Hormuz for exports. All 2.5 Mbpd of exports halted. Mina al-Ahmadi and Mina Abdullah terminals idle. No alternative pipeline routes. Strategic reserves being consumed domestically. Massive revenue loss.

Unaffected by Middle East conflict. US considering temporary sanctions relief to boost Venezuelan output. However, decades of underinvestment limit surge capacity to ~150,000 bpd additional within 6 months. Heavy crude requires specialized refining.

hormuzImpact.insuranceDetail: Lloyd's of London war-risk premium jumped from 0.03% to 1.5% of hull value. Most underwriters have suspended coverage entirely for Persian Gulf transit. Effective insurance cost for a laden VLCC rose from ~$50,000 to ~$2.1 million per voyage.
hormuzImpact.reroutingDetail: Alternative route via Cape of Good Hope adds approximately 12 days and 6,400 nautical miles to Middle East-to-Europe shipments. Middle East-to-Asia routes add ~8 days via Lombok Strait. Additional fuel costs of $800,000-$1.2 million per VLCC voyage.

affectedCountries.alternativeRoutes: East-West Pipeline to Yanbu (Red Sea) — limited to 5 Mbpd
Habshan-Fujairah pipeline — 1.5 Mbpd, under Houthi threat
None — fully landlocked from export routes
None — LNG terminals face same blockade
Jask terminal (Gulf of Oman) — damaged in strikes

hormuzImpact.description: Strait of Hormuz effectively closed (Day 17). 24+ vessels damaged, 3 sunk since war began. Only 21 tankers total have transited since war began — traffic down 97%. 400+ ships stranded. IMO negotiating humanitarian corridor to free ~20,000 seafarers. 7-nation Hormuz coalition formed (UK, France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, Japan, Canada). Brent $108.65 (hit $119 intraday), WTI $96.14. VLCC charter rates at $800K/day.

consumerImpact.note: National weighted averages. Regional prices vary significantly — California, Hawaii, and Northeast typically 15-30% above national average.

fuels.detail: Gas at $3.88/gal (March 19 AAA). Up 28¢ in one week. California at $5.62/gal, Oklahoma cheapest at $3.24. Brent at $108.65 after $119 spike. Fed holds rates at 3.5-3.75%. Gas Prices Relief Act of 2026 introduced to suspend 18.4c/gal federal gas tax.
National average at $5.07/gal — up sharply from pre-war levels. Highest since 2022. Critical for trucking and freight — fuel surcharges being implemented across major carriers.
Airlines implementing emergency fuel surcharges of $50-150 per ticket. Several carriers reducing flight frequencies by 10-20%. Trans-Pacific and Middle East routes most affected.
Primarily affects Northeast US households (5.3 million homes). Late-winter timing means many households have low tank levels. State emergency heating assistance programs activated in 12 states.

householdImpact.detail: Average US household of 4 facing an estimated $285/month increase in energy-related costs. Lower-income households (bottom quintile) spending 22% of income on energy, up from 14%.

keyFacts.fact: 21 million barrels per day of oil transit is blocked through the Strait of Hormuz
keyFacts.detail: The Strait of Hormuz is the world's most critical oil chokepoint. At its narrowest, shipping lanes are only 3 km wide in each direction. Iran has deployed naval mines, fast-attack boats, and shore-based anti-ship missiles along the strait. The 21 Mbpd figure includes crude oil, refined products, and LNG equivalent volumes.

keyFacts.fact: IEA approves record 400 million barrel emergency oil release — largest coordinated drawdown in history
keyFacts.detail: On March 11, IEA member nations agreed to release 400 million barrels from strategic reserves over 60 days — the largest coordinated release in IEA history, surpassing the 2022 release of 182M barrels during the Russia-Ukraine crisis. The US SPR was at 415.4 million barrels before the release. Maximum combined release rate across all IEA members is ~6 Mbpd, far less than the ~15 Mbpd blocked by Hormuz.
keyFacts.source: IEA Emergency Response — Oil Stock Levels

keyFacts.fact: The last comparable supply disruption was the 1973 Arab Oil Embargo, which removed only 4.3 Mbpd from global supply
keyFacts.detail: The current Hormuz blockade has removed approximately 15 Mbpd of net supply (accounting for pipeline alternatives) — more than triple the 1973 embargo. The 1973 event caused a 267% price increase and triggered a global recession. The 1979 Iranian Revolution removed ~5.6 Mbpd. No disruption in history approaches the current scale.

keyFacts.fact: OPEC spare capacity of ~4.5 Mbpd cannot reach markets due to the Hormuz blockade
keyFacts.detail: Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Iraq hold the bulk of OPEC's spare production capacity. However, most of this capacity is located on the Persian Gulf coast behind the Hormuz blockade. Only Saudi Arabia's East-West Pipeline to Yanbu (Red Sea coast) offers a bypass, and its 5 Mbpd capacity is already operating near maximum. Net accessible spare capacity is estimated at only 1.5 Mbpd.

keyFacts.fact: War-risk insurance for Persian Gulf tankers has increased by 340%, effectively halting commercial shipping
keyFacts.detail: Lloyd's of London and the International Group of P&I Clubs have either suspended coverage or raised war-risk premiums from 0.03% to 1.5% of hull value. For a typical VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) valued at $120 million, this means premiums of $1.8 million per transit versus the previous ~$36,000. Most ship operators have deemed the risk uninsurable and withdrawn all vessels from the Persian Gulf.
keyFacts.source: Lloyd's List Intelligence — War Risk Assessment

keyFacts.fact: US gasoline prices at $3.88/gallon — up 22.0% from pre-war $3.18/gal. Highest since Sep 2023. California at $5.62/gal.
keyFacts.detail: Gas at $3.88/gal (March 19 AAA). Up 28¢ in one week. California highest at $5.62, Oklahoma cheapest at $3.24. Brent at $108.65. Fed holds rates at 3.5-3.75%; inflation forecast raised to ~3% through late 2026. Gas Prices Relief Act of 2026 introduced to suspend 18.4c/gal federal gas tax.
keyFacts.source: EIA Weekly Retail Gasoline and Diesel Prices

keyFacts.fact: Brent crude at $108.65/barrel (+36.2% from pre-war) and WTI at $96.14 (+27.3%). Brent spiked to $119 intraday on Ras Laffan LNG strike before settling.
keyFacts.detail: Brent spiked to $119 on Iran's strike on Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG facility, then pulled back to $108.65 after Netanyahu said Israel would help reopen Hormuz. Dubai crude hit record $166.80/barrel. European jet fuel at $220/barrel. Goldman Sachs warns Brent could surpass 2008's $147.50 all-time high.

keyFacts.fact: US Treasury waives Russia oil sanctions for 1 month to ease global supply crunch amid Hormuz blockade.
keyFacts.detail: On Day 14, the US Treasury Department temporarily waived sanctions on Russian oil exports for 30 days in an effort to increase global oil supply while the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed. The waiver acknowledges the severity of the supply disruption and the limits of the IEA reserve release. Gas prices at $3.68/gal nationally (AAA).

keyFacts.fact: IEA authorizes record 400M barrel coordinated reserve release — US contributing 172M from SPR. Prices remain above $100 despite release.

Fed meeting March 17-18 — 99% probability holds rates at 3.50-3.75%; inflation complicated by war
Trump weighing seizure of Kharg Island — would be 'economic knockout of the regime' (FT interview)

Treasury Secretary Bessent says US will allow Iranian oil tankers through Hormuz — oil drops 4-6% on the news
WSJ reports US building multinational naval escort coalition for Strait of Hormuz commercial shipping
Dubai crude hits all-time record $166.80/barrel — physical oil market pricing far above futures. European jet fuel at $220/barrel, diesel at $200/barrel.
Goldman Sachs warns Brent could surpass 2008 all-time high of $147.50 if outages persist. Brent has surged ~80% since war began Feb 28.

Bessent: US may unsanction ~140M barrels of Iranian oil stranded on tankers to lower prices for 10-14 days. 'Using Iranian barrels against the Iranians.' Also flagged potential additional SPR release beyond IEA's 400M.
